title: Priming Sugar Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Priming Sugar for Home Beer Carbonation
description: Calculate grams of sugar to carbonate your homebrew beer based on CO₂ volumes and temperature. 6–8 g/L dextrose for standard beers. Free calculator.
intro: **Priming sugar** is the sugar you add before bottling so residual yeast generates CO₂ and carbonates the beer in the bottle. It depends on volume, target CO₂ volumes, and peak fermentation temperature. This calculator uses John Palmer's standard formula for dextrose, table sugar, DME, or honey.
keyTakeaway: **6–8 g/L dextrose for standard beers (2.4 vol CO₂)**. Belgians 9–10 g/L, lagers 6–7, stouts 4–5. Too much = exploding bottles.
useCases: You're about to bottle and don't know how much sugar to add.
You want a medium IPA or high-carbonation Belgian with maximum fizz.
You switched from dextrose to table sugar and need to adjust the amount.
Your last batch came out flat.
You want to switch to force carbonation in a keg.
explanation:
## What Is Priming Sugar

When bottling, you add sugar that residual yeast consumes, generating CO₂ in the sealed bottle. This takes **2–3 weeks** at 68°F (20°C).

## CO₂ Volumes by Beer Style

| Style | Volumes |
|---|---|
| British Cask Ale | 1.0–1.5 |
| Porter, Stout | 1.7–2.3 |
| **American Ale, IPA** | **2.2–2.7** |
| Pilsner, Lager | 2.4–2.7 |
| Hefeweizen, Wheat | 3.3–4.5 |
| Belgian Tripel, Saison | 3.0–4.0 |

## The Formula

```
grams = (target_vol − residual_vol) × volume_L × factor

dextrose_factor = 3.86
table_sugar_factor = 3.51
DME_factor = 4.50
honey_factor = 4.26

residual_vol = 3.0378 − 0.050062 × T(°C) + 0.00026555 × T²
```

## Quick Reference for 20L

| Volumes | Dextrose | Table Sugar |
|---|---|---|
| 2.0 (stout) | 85g | 77g |
| **2.4 (IPA)** | **120g** | **110g** |
| 2.7 (pilsner) | 145g | 130g |
| 3.0 (wheat) | 170g | 155g |
| 3.5 (Belgian) | 205g | 185g |

## Warning: Bottle Bombs

>4 vol CO₂ in standard bottles has **explosion risk**. Use champagne or Belgian bottles for high carbonation.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Forget residual CO₂**: fermentation at 82°F (28°C) = 0.6 vol, at 59°F (15°C) = 1.1 vol.
2. **Don't boil the priming sugar**: make a 5-minute syrup to sterilize.
3. **Shaking when bottling**: oxidizes the beer.

See [final volume calculator](/calculadora-final-volume-cerveza-fermentador).

faq.q: What's the difference between dextrose and table sugar for priming?
faq.a: Both work. Dextrose ferments cleaner; table sugar is ~0.9% less efficient. Use whichever you have.

faq.q: How long does beer take to carbonate after bottling?
faq.a: 2–3 weeks at 68°F (20°C). Colder temperatures take 4+ weeks.

faq.q: Can beer bottles really explode from carbonation?
faq.a: Yes, over 4 volumes CO₂ in standard bottles risks breakage. Keep to 2.4–2.7 vol and use proper bottles.

faq.q: Do I need to boil the priming sugar?
faq.a: Yes. Heat the sugar for 5 minutes to sterilize and prevent infection.

faq.q: What CO₂ volumes should different beer styles have?
faq.a: Stouts 1.7–2.3, IPAs 2.2–2.7, Pilsners 2.4–2.7, Belgians 3.0–4.0, Wheats 3.3–4.5 volumes.

faq.q: What's residual CO₂ and why does it matter?
faq.a: It's CO₂ already in the beer from fermentation, determined by temperature. You must subtract it from your target to avoid over-carbonation.

faq.q: Why did my beer come out flat?
faq.a: Too little priming, low temperature during conditioning, dead yeast, or poor bottle seals. Wait 3+ weeks at 68°F and check conditions.

faq.q: Can I use honey instead of dextrose for priming?
faq.a: Yes, but use 10% more than dextrose equivalent since honey is 75–80% fermentable.
